Uniface 10: Concepts

Uniface 10 introduces new concepts and terminology that are fundamental to understanding the Uniface IDE.

The most important concepts are discussed below. For a summary of terminology changes, see Uniface 10: Changes in Terminology.

Development Objects and Main Development Objects

Uniface has long had the concept of development objects and runtime objects, but Uniface 10 introduces the concept of Main development objects.

Development objects are application definitions for logical application units, such as components, fields, properties, messages, variables, and so on. During the build process, development objects are compiled into executable runtime objects and libraries, which can be packaged into archives for immediate deployment.

Main development objects are logical aggregations of development objects that are treated as a logical unit for editing and version control. Main development objects include modeled entities, components, and typed libraries. Each main development object has an associated editor that is used to edit both the main object and its sub-objects.

For more information, see Uniface Development Objects.

Modeled Objects and Derived Objects

Modeled objects are development objects that can be inherited by other development objects, which are typically created using the modeled object. Derived objects inherit from modeled objects. Thus a component entity that is created using a modeled entity is a derived entity.

In previous versions of Uniface, only objects belonging to the application model were occasionally referred to as modeled objects. In Uniface 10, the term has been expanded and made more consistent. Modeled objects now comprise:

  • Modeled entities and fields—entity and field definitions that define the data that the application accesses, as well as non-database objects such as reusable buttons.
  • Modeled properties—these are named values of selected entity and field properties, such as the Field Interface, Field Layout, and Field Syntax properties. in Uniface 9, they were known as templates.
  • Modeled components—these are components that are used to create other components with the same component-level properties and ProcScript. in Uniface 9, they were known as component templates.

The application model no longer exists as a separate object. Instead, it is a mandatory part of the modeled entity name and identifies the entity's namespace. However, the term application model can still be used to refer to a collection of modeled objects that share the same namespace.

Templates and Palettes

Templates are development objects that can be copied to create new development objects. Once they are copied, there is no link between the original template and the object that is based on it. If the template is changed, objects that were created with the template do not inherit the change.

In Uniface 9, the term templates was used for a variety of objects, some of which were copied (field templates) and others that were inherited (component and property templates).

In the Uniface 10, the terms have been changed to make this distinction clear.

  • An object that is reused rather than copied is a modeled object and is made available to other objects in the Model tab of the Resource Browser.
  • An object that is copied to create a new object is a template, and is made available in the Templates tab of the Resource Browser.

Uniface 10 provides a rich set of templates that you can use to explicitly create new objects. It is also possible to define and use your own templates, which can be used instead of or in addition to the ones provided by Uniface.

Note: In Uniface 9, a similar mechanism, known as theUniface defaults, was implicitly used to create all new objects. Definitions in the defaults could be customized to suit your own requirements but it was not possible to define an alternative set of default templates. This is now possible.

Uniface 10 also introduces the concept of a palette, which is main development object that serves as a container for templates. For more information, see Templates and Palettes.

Projects

Projects are main development objects used to create and organize other main development objects. Projects are used to create new modeled entities, components, some libraries, and other projects. For more information, see Projects.

Libraries

In previous versions of Uniface, libraries were untyped, meaning that a library contained a heterogeneous collection of different types of runtime objects, such as messages, menus, and global ProcScripts.

Uniface 10 introduces typed libraries, which can contain only one type of object or resource. For example, a snippet library only contains code snippets, a menu library only contains menu objects for desktop applications.

Libraries of different types, may have the same name, which is treated as a shared namespace when referenced by $variation.

For more information, see Libraries.
